title: Fitting an automatic pop-hole opener
subtitle: How to install a light-sensor or timer-operated door that opens and closes the coop without you.
excerpt: A pop-hole opener that works by itself is not a convenience gadget. It is a fox-loss prevention tool. Most fox attacks happen in the hour before dawn and the hour after dusk. An automatic opener closes the coop at dusk and opens it at dawn, at the right time, every day, whether you are there or not.
sourceNotes: DEFRA, Code of Practice for the Welfare of Laying Hens (2018). RSPCA guidance on housing and enrichment for chickens.

glossaryTerms.definition: The small door at ground level in a chicken coop through which hens enter and leave. Typically 20–30 cm wide and 30–40 cm tall for a standard layer. The primary access point that foxes and other predators attempt to breach at night.

content.text: opener typically uses either a light sensor (opens at a set light level at dawn, closes at dusk) or a programmable timer. Both work well. A light sensor adjusts automatically as the year turns, closing later in summer and earlier in winter without any reprogramming. A timer requires you to adjust it as the seasons change but gives precise control and is unaffected by overcast days.

The opener unit, batteries or a power supply, and either a sliding door panel or the motor attachment for your existing door. Most units come with an aluminium sliding door, a motor and cable, a fixing bracket, and a sensor or timer module. Read the unit's instructions before measuring your pop hole; the panel dimensions vary between manufacturers.

Measure the existing pop hole width and height. The aluminium door panel needs to be wider and taller than the opening it covers. Mark the channel positions on either side of the pop hole; the door panel slides vertically in two channels. The bottom of the channels should be flush with the floor inside the coop or just below it (if there is a step up, the door needs to close on the step, not below it where it could gap).

Fix the two guide channels vertically on either side of the pop hole, parallel and spaced to the door panel's width. Use screws that grip the coop timbers, not nails. Slide the door panel into the channels and check it moves freely. The motor mounts above the channels on a bracket; the cable from the motor attaches to a small fitting at the top of the door panel. Follow the manufacturer's cable attachment instructions; most use a looped cord rather than a rigid link so the door does not jam if a hen is caught in it.

attrs.title: Anti-entrapment safety
attrs.body: Most current units include a load sensor or obstacle detection that stops and reverses the door if it meets resistance during closing. Check that the unit you have fitted has this feature. Test it by placing your hand lightly in the door's path as it closes; it should stop and reverse within a few millimetres of contact. If your unit does not have obstacle detection, observe the flock going to roost for the first few evenings to confirm all birds are inside before dusk.

content.text: For a timer: set the closing time 30 minutes after the average roosting time of your flock. In midsummer this may be 21:30; in midwinter 16:00. Set opening time 20 minutes after civil dawn. Adjust the close time monthly in spring and autumn.

For a light sensor: most units allow you to set a sensitivity threshold. Start in the middle of the range and observe for a week. If the door closes while birds are still outside, reduce the sensitivity so it triggers later. If it opens before the birds have come off roost, wait until you observe a few natural open and close cycles before adjusting.

An automatic opener does not replace the daily visit; it removes the time-critical elements of it. Check the door has opened and closed on each visit. Check the battery level indicator monthly; a flat battery in January leaves the coop open all night. Most units have a visual indicator; some send alerts to a connected app.
